stem: A 44-year-old client receiving fresh frozen plasma (FFP) for coagulopathy develops sudden respiratory distress two hours into the infusion. The nurse documents the findings in the electronic health record. Based on the documentation, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the nurse for the 44-year-old client in room 3B. At 14:30, about two hours into her second unit of FFP, she developed sudden, severe respiratory distress.
Background: The client has a history of liver cirrhosis and was receiving FFP for coagulopathy, with a baseline INR of 2.0.
Assessment: She is hypotensive at 88/52 mmHg, tachycardic at 128/min, with a new fever of 38.8°C. Her respiratory rate is 34/min, and SpO2 is 74% on a 6L nasal cannula with diffuse bilateral crackles on auscultation.
Recommendation: I have stopped the transfusion. I suspect Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI) and need an immediate provider evaluation. I have also ordered a STAT portable chest x-ray.

tabs.title: Pulmonary & Hemodynamic Log
tabs.content:
AssessmentFindingSignificance
Blood Pressure88/52 mmHg (Low)Points to TRALI
Temperature101.8°F (Fever)Inflammatory response
Lung SoundsDiffuse bilateral cracklesNon-cardiogenic edema
CVP (if monitored)3 mmHg (Normal/Low)Not volume overload

actionOptions.text: Stop the transfusion and notify the provider and blood bank.
Provide aggressive respiratory support (O2 / CPAP / Intubation).
Lower the head of the bed to the flat position.

hoverRationales.c1: This is incorrect. Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO) is caused by fluid volume overload, which typically presents with hypertension and signs of heart failure, not the hypotension and fever seen in this client.
hoverRationales.c2: This is correct. Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI) is an inflammatory response to donor antibodies. It is characterized by acute respiratory distress, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, hypotension, and fever within six hours of transfusion.
hoverRationales.c3: This is incorrect. While a severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis) can cause hypotension and respiratory distress, it typically involves urticaria, pruritus, or angioedema, which are not present. Fever is also less common.
hoverRationales.a1: This is correct. Stopping the transfusion is the immediate priority to prevent further exposure to the causative agent in the donor plasma. This action limits the progression of the inflammatory lung injury.
hoverRationales.a2: This is correct. The client's severe hypoxemia (SpO2 74%) indicates acute respiratory failure. Providing immediate, high-level respiratory support is critical to correct life-threatening hypoxemia and reduce the work of breathing.
hoverRationales.a3: This is incorrect. Administering a diuretic is contraindicated in TRALI because the client is already hypotensive. Furosemide would worsen hypotension and could precipitate shock. Diuretics are appropriate for TACO.
hoverRationales.a4: This is incorrect. Lowering the head of the bed is contraindicated for a client in respiratory distress. This position increases the work of breathing and can worsen hypoxemia.
hoverRationales.p1: This is correct. TRALI involves a systemic inflammatory response causing vasodilation and capillary leak, leading to hypotension and fever. Monitoring these parameters is crucial for assessing hemodynamic stability and the severity of the reaction.
hoverRationales.p2: This is correct. The pathophysiology of TRALI involves acute inflammation and increased permeability of pulmonary capillaries, causing non-cardiogenic edema. Monitoring oxygenation and lung sounds directly assesses the extent of this lung injury.
hoverRationales.p3: This is incorrect. While urine output is an important indicator of perfusion, it is not a primary parameter to monitor during the immediate management of TRALI. The priority is stabilizing cardiorespiratory function.
hoverRationales.p4: This is incorrect. Measuring abdominal girth is relevant for monitoring ascites related to the client's liver cirrhosis. It is not a priority parameter for managing an acute, life-threatening transfusion reaction.
